text:
Everything in life involves compromise. Occasionally I drive over 200 miles a day for work so when looking for my first EV I wanted one with a range of 250 miles or more. I settled for one with 200 miles (real world) range because I figured so long as I had the range to do my work I could take the time and money hit of charging to get home. Almost a year later I still haven't needed to charge away from home and the leccy is 1/3 the price of Diesel.

text:
The charger situation is very very variable. For the first 2 years I didn't use my home charger because ChargePlace Scotland units were free. Then as EVs took off you started to see queues and now they have tariffs so I moved to using my home charger.

In the last three years the numerous CPS rapid chargers have been joined by an mfg ev hub (12 250 kW units), a forev hub (4 250 kW units), BP pulse 4 350 kW units, pogo are building another 6 250 kW units and that's within 3 miles of my house. And if you go up to 10 miles away there's 2 more mfg hubs, 2 new tesla superchargers an Arnold Clark hub etc. So the reality here isn't the same as down south that you're seeing. 

Otherwise a Cracking rebuttal of all the usual anti ev nonsense. Oh and it's not just Vauxhall, the Škoda Elroq is also the same price as the Škoda Karoq (the similar ice car in the Škoda range)

text: I tend to pay close attention to companies that sit upstream in emerging industries because supply chain positioning often matters more than the finished product branding. Talga Group has caught my attention for that reason. The company is focused on developing graphite anode materials used inside lithium ion batteries, which are a core component of electric vehicle technology. As more countries move toward electrification and localized battery production, upstream materials suppliers can become strategically important pieces of that ecosystem. When I compare Talga to other advanced materials and graphene related names in the market, it appears aligned with where long term demand may develop. It is still a growth stage company and there are execution and financing risks involved, but from a structural supply chain perspective, it is one I believe is worth researching further. This is simply my personal opinion and not financial advice.

text:
Not all green plate in China are EV. Green plate can be "EV" or "hybird vehicle"
In most case, you can tell the difference by looking at the thrid letter.

Take the vehicle plate (粵ADF2271) at 0:08 as an example.
粵 means Guangdong province.
A stands for provincial capital city of Guangzhou.
D means its all electirc. (where F means hybird).

In conclusion, for green plate, in most case, third letter can tell if its EV or hybird.
But sometimes in cities which ran out of combination, the third letter can be different.

text:
1:52 - the 334 miles displayed on the Tesla “charging” screen here is NOT what the trip computer thinks the car can do. It's simply the rated (US EPA) consumption x percentage of battery filled. It is not accounting for the current weather, the route entered, or your personal driving style. It's essentially just an alternate way of displaying the state of charge percentage (which is why it's in the charging menu, not in the trip planner or the consumption info).

What you should have been looking at is the estimate given in the Consumption app.

I doubt this was done to intentionally be deceiving, but it just shows why EVs need to be reviewed by people that are familiar with the systems on board.

I suspect the same mistake might have been made on some of the other cars in this video too.

text:
The Audi should approximately  have been given the same amount of energy in the tank as the electric cars then this test is fair. 

Tesla 75kWh, Kia 78kWh, Skoda 77kWh, Xpeng 84 kWh, BYD 91 kWh and Leapmotor 70 kWh makes an average of 79 kWh which is 284 MJ(megajoules)
Depending on blend and quality 1 gallon of gasoline contains 131.7 MJ which would allow the petrol car 2.2(2.156) Gallons of patrol 
So if you allow the petrol car the same amount of energy as all the other cars in this test according to the average miles a gallon shown here 47:04 at 25.2 mpg the actual range of the petrol car in this test would be 2.2 x 25.2 = ~55.44 miles if you would drive the car completely empty.

text:
I have 3 EVs and 1 gas vehicle. The gas vehicle is there for holding my flame thrower fuel. Lot easier to jack down a battery pack and repair modules or even replace it than it is to hoist an engine out and drop in a crate. I've done both. I enjoyed my time melting tires in gas cars but gas cars have reach point of diminishing returns.

As for the fire risk - the word you're looking for is incidence. It's the total occurrences during a given time period divided by total possibilities. So while there are many gas car, there are a higher ratio of gas car fires than EV fire ratio. Actuaries look at those numbers when modeling risk rates. It varies by model, but EVs generally catch on fire thus far at about 1/5th the rate combustion cars do. When they do ignite it is much more difficult to put EV fires out due to higher burn temps and self fueling (batteries are their own oxider, fuel and heat source). If you live close to a fire station this might matter since it could mean the difference between part of your home vs all of your home burning. If you're not in a 2 min response window your home is toast no matter if it was a horse that kicked over a kerosene lamp in the barn or an EV fire. Most people don't assess risk properly, otherwise they would actually bother to check their tire pressures instead of worrying about fires beforehand.

text: In my opinion, the EV market will go no where until there is a standards body that mandates uniformity across all EVs in regards to the batteries and charging.  One of the clips said an F150 still has a 36 gallon tank regardless of how old it is and an EV battery doesn't give out the same amount of stored power as it ages.  But it goes further than that.  Gas is gas.  I can use current gas to run a 1950s car as I can with a current model ICE car.  You can't say that with EVs.  Even within the current manufacturer, there has been instances where you can't get a replacement battery.  I mention the standards body because something needs to be done to make the electrical connecters for both charging and for the battery connections to be uniform.  With many battery operated devices around the house, you can have confidence you can get any AA battery to put into the device and it will work.  Not with an EV.

text: I leased a new 2018 Nissan LEAF S and tried that 120 volt extension cord trick, but only 25 feet to reach from the garage. The cord got very  hot, and the owners manual warned not to use ANY extension cords. My LEAF also had a 50 amp 240 volt plug and I installed a NEMA 14-50 outlet to charge SAFELY in about 5 hours. It didn't really matter because the range dropped in half when the temperature was 20 degrees. That's right, the 150 mile range turned into 75 miles and became useless driving anywhere out of town. My friendly Nissan dealer blamed me for "operating the vehicle improperly". Fortunately, Carmax bought it after only 3 weeks. I lost $7,500 but I learned a painful lesson and got rid of a car I could only drive in moderately warm weather.

text:
16:04 Steer-by-wire built by the lowest bidder is a recipe for serious injury.
True, aircraft have been using fly by wire for decades now, but aircraft have strict FAA regulations to follow.  Even the most minor corrosion calls for replacement of the part or service.  There are routine checks that happen on strict service intervals.  The aircraft is stripped down, inspected, and serviced.  What's more, each part needs to meet a certain exacting specification.  Be it torque yield, material composition, or physical dimensions.  Certain parts have substitutions, but those are non-structural or mechanical.  An aircraft will be grounded for irregular wear on a stabilizer bushing.

Until we have this level of scrutiny for vehicles, steer-by-wire is a disaster waiting to happen.
clean_text: 16:04 steer-by-wire built by the lowest bidder is a recipe for serious injury. true, aircraft have been using fly by wire for decades now, but aircraft have strict faa regulations to follow. even the most minor corrosion calls for replacement of the part or service. there are routine checks that happen on strict service intervals. the aircraft is stripped down, inspected, and serviced. what's more, each part needs to meet a certain exacting specification. be it torque yield, material composition, or physical dimensions. certain parts have substitutions, but those are non-structural or mechanical. an aircraft will be grounded for irregular wear on a stabilizer bushing. until we have this level of scrutiny for vehicles, steer-by-wire is a disaster waiting to happen.

text: Regarding the catching on fire thing. The difference between a gas car and a battery car is obvious. An electric car doesn't need air to operate and for it to go 500 miles, has to carry all the energy it would take to go that distance. A gasoline car carries only part of the energy because it burns oxygen found in air. Remove of air and a fire will go out. A full tank of gas cannot wholly be lit on fire, you would need to spread it out and let all the fuel get exposed to air to get it to burn. So while a gas car may catch on fire, those fires can be extinguished and the fires are usually significantly less violent and more controllable.
clean_text: regarding the catching on fire thing. the difference between a gas car and a battery car is obvious. an electric car doesn't need air to operate and for it to go 500 miles, has to carry all the energy it would take to go that distance. a gasoline car carries only part of the energy because it burns oxygen found in air. remove of air and a fire will go out. a full tank of gas cannot wholly be lit on fire, you would need to spread it out and let all the fuel get exposed to air to get it to burn. so while a gas car may catch on fire, those fires can be extinguished and the fires are usually significantly less violent and more controllable.

text:
3rd winter in Chicago with an Ioniq 5 and just cracked 80,000 miles in 2.5 years.  Yes, the range drops about 20-25%, it was about 35% drop on a -12º day, but that is all superceded by the fact you just plug in overnight.  Even with nearly triple the average US driving we have charged at a public charger 6 times total.    I did the math the other day and have saved 21 hours of pumping gas at the gas station (if you figure 5 minutes for going to the gas station, pumping, and getting back on the road, which I would say is probably conservative). 21 hours is a long time to be pumping gas. As long as you can charge at home, I don't ever see buying another gas car in our family.  My next vehicle is the Rivian R2.
In our Minivan that mileage would equate to 3,478 gallons, which is about half of a semi-tanker that fills the gas stations.  That is pretty crazy when you think about it in the bigger picture view than just the oh, I got 12 gallons of gas.

text: Having grown up in the bitter cold, the range thing is terrifying. First thought was winter storms can mean extended power outages, so that means with a small generator or battery backup, I'd have to choose between the appliances or the car. The real scary thing to me is the very real scenario of being stuck on the highway in a storm moving at 5-10 mph or ending up having to pull over for the night and sleeping in the car. That is 100% doable with minimal planning with a gas/diesel vehicle, as long as you have half a tank. The idea of the battery draining on the side of the road, hours from civilization, in the bitter cold is awful.

At 80% charge, even on a really cold day I typically have 135 miles of range, which is almost twice my commute. Even in a gas car you're still getting a range drop in cold weather, you just don't pay as close attention to it. text:
Not only can you preheat in the garage, but you can schedule preheating/conditioning to occur every day, based on departure times. This was possible in my 2014 Leaf and in every EV and most PHEV's I've looked at or owned.

I take my daughter to the bus stop every morning before work, and in Minnesota it's been 0F or lower a lot of mornings this year, but I have it scheduled for 10 minutes after she gets picked up so it's warm when we take the car, when I leave for work, and even keeps the heat on if I turn it off while at the bus stop. It's all automatic, I don't even think of it. And if I'm plugged in when it first warms up, despite my charger being outside the garage, it has warmed up using the home power with little to no battery drain.

I only have an EV and it gets really cold here in Alberta.  NJ cold minus 20F.    Range is half but heat is fast, even street parking is fine if you can block heater plug in.  Level 1 at 8 amps works fine.  

Road trips are different in EVs.  You plan your trip stop for lunch or dinner breaks, while you charge.  Many chargers are close to or built in at restaurants.  

It is all about snow tires.  

Ironically highway driving uses less power than city streets in cold winters when you run the heater constantly.  At -30F you use 13 kW to drive at 62 mph and 7 kW for the heater.  At 31 mph you use 5 kW to drive and 7 for the heater.  One uses 20 kWh to go 62 miles the other uses 24 kWh to go the same 62 miles.

text:
I drive a 24 Equinox EV, and its performance is cut in half during the coldest winter months. I'd say our average cold day in the winter is about -20 C / -4 F. However, we get at least 20-30 days of -30 C / -22F. I also drive a long distance for work. 171 KM / 106 M in distance round-trip. 

On the coldest days, driving 171 KM I charge to 100% and get home with about 10% left. The car's range is about 450 KM / 279 M on a full charge. Meaning, I drive only 171 KM, but because of the extreme temperature, it is as if I have driven 400 KM. 

Based on climate and distance traveled, I know this is an extreme case. Regardless, I do not regret the purchase. I would get the vehicle again.

text:
OK, here in the UK the average temp during winter is around 4 ○C. My EV range drops around 40 miles or so. 
Thing is, I work 48 weeks in the year, 47 miles to and 47 miles from home. 

I go on 4 journeys per year of around 530 miles. The first 220 miles are virtually free (circa £5) the remainder costs around £0.27  per mile thereonafter. Driving in the uk, you will average around 47 miles per hour, so three hours of driving gets you around 141 miles. Break for 10 minutes, charge whilst on your break and you will have enough for a further 200 miles. So two 10 min breaks gets you all the way. This is the very same way, I drive an ICE car. I do have two cars, but in 4 months or so, my 5 year old ICE car gets traded in for a new mini jcw electric with even less range than my EV6.  

Those who know Evs, know that bullshit is still bullshit.

text: Dont get me wrong... I love EVs. But, I also love combustion cars. All of these features mentioned in this video (minus the traction control stuff, maybe) could be put in an ICE vehicle. None of the components needed for most of these features couldn't be in a gas vehicle, they just dont put them in because we already have working components that leverage the rotational force of the engine using belts and pulleys.
